guideline_references: RACGP: aRD (autoimmune rheumatic disease) shared-care principles
Australian Rheumatology Association: methotrexate patient information and monitoring
eTG: Rheumatology — fibromyalgia and centralised pain management
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: alarming framing (sudden severe widespread pain, swollen tight hands, profound immobility, a source 'Urgency Justification' invoking acute inflammatory joint destruction / systemic infection / urgent steroid) BUT the source's own acute-red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (no single hot red maximally-swollen joint, no high fever/rigors, no malar rash/pleuritic pain/renal signs) and the in-person disposition is routine (GP review in 1 week). Correct telehealth answer is to recognise a flare of a KNOWN benign chronic condition, tier T2 (prompt GP), and attach PROMINENT mandatory safety-netting — not to escalate to ED/000.

Red herring: mild bilateral MCP synovitis + the word 'swelling' + the urgency framing are distractors toward septic arthritis / acute rheumatological crisis; the discriminating features (polyarticular, mild, symmetric, no fever, allodynia far beyond joints) point back to a centralised-pain flare.

Immunosuppression modifier: patient is on weekly methotrexate — this genuinely LOWERS the threshold for the fever/hot-joint safety-net (septic arthritis and sepsis risk), so the safety-netting must be robust even though baseline tier stays low.

Telehealth reprojection: an in-person home visit with clinician-measured vitals and a hands-on joint/trigger-point examination; the AI Doctor cannot palpate joints, test power, or measure BP, so those findings are sealed and the AI is scored on recognise + prompt GP referral + hydration advice + safety-net.
Social/hydration risk: lives alone, bedbound tonight, inadequate fluid intake — a live deterioration pathway that raises the safety-net floor without raising the baseline tier.
Age and sex inferred (not stated in source) — flagged.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), source note CFE-015.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
